Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent this semester. I'd set aside time to study, maybe around 29 hours a week on paper, but a lot of that was me sitting with materials open without really absorbing much. I preferred reading and writing things out, so I'd often print stuff or copy notes into documents. The routine existed in theory, but I wasn't always mentally present during those hours.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot, though not always because of deadlines. Sometimes I'd just lose track of what was due when, especially with 11 online courses to juggle. I'd have bursts of activity when I remembered something was coming up, then long stretches where I'd barely touch the course. It wasn't strategic—more like reactive chaos that I kept telling myself I'd fix next week.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I got an email notification or when a peer mentioned something about an assignment in our group chat. The peer support group I was connected with would sometimes remind me about deadlines, which helped. Without those external prompts, I could go days without thinking about logging in. It wasn't that I didn't care—I just didn't have that internal drive pushing me there.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a couple weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, checking in every morning. But that fell apart pretty quickly. The LMS never became automatic for me—it always felt like something I had to consciously decide to do, and most days I just didn't make that decision.
Q5: The reading materials and written guides were most useful to me since that's how I learn best. I'd download PDFs and actually engage with those more than the videos. The discussion boards were occasionally helpful when peers explained concepts in different ways. But honestly, I got more value from the materials I could take offline and work through at my own pace.
Q6: I mostly ignored the videos—43 minutes total over the whole course, which is embarrassing to admit. They just didn't match how I learn, and I'd zone out watching them. I also delayed a lot of the optional readings and practice activities, always thinking I'd get to them later. The discussion posts I did were minimal because I never felt like I had anything smart to contribute.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to an assignment or if it helped me understand something I was genuinely confused about. When my peers in the support group recommended something specific, I'd prioritize it. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to my grade, I'd skip it without much guilt.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. With required ones, there was at least some external pressure, even if I didn't always follow through. Optional stuff? I'd tell myself it was extra, and with my low motivation, extra just didn't happen. It's like my brain needed the threat of consequences to even consider engaging.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline with downloaded PDFs, notes I'd written out by hand, and sometimes textbooks I found elsewhere. I'd read through materials multiple times away from my computer. The LMS was more like a delivery system for content that I'd then engage with separately, in ways that felt more natural to me.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours with printed notes, re-reading and annotating, working through concepts on paper. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also discuss course content with peers over coffee or through text messages—real learning conversations that the LMS had no way of tracking. My engagement looked worse than it actually was.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my standard approach. I'd download a PDF or reading once, then work with it for days without going back to the LMS. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could study without internet distractions. One download might represent hours of subsequent study time that never got recorded.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I was surprised by how quickly I fell behind despite thinking I was keeping up. My attendance dropped significantly, and I started avoiding the course altogether. The peer support helped a bit, but I was already in a hole. When you're already failing, it's hard to find the motivation to climb out.
Q13: Managing 11 online courses was overwhelming, even with high resources available to me. Fatigue definitely played a role—I'd sit down to study and just feel empty. The workload made me prioritize other courses where I felt I had a better chance of passing. This course kept getting pushed down my list until it was too late.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd open readings and scroll through without really processing anything. My eyes would move across the page but my mind was elsewhere. Sometimes I'd log in, click around to feel productive, then log out having learned nothing. It was engagement theater for my own conscience.
Q15: I postponed the bigger assignments constantly, even knowing they mattered. The exam especially—I kept telling myself I'd prepare properly, but I never did. I think part of it was fear of confirming that I didn't understand the material. Avoidance felt safer than facing how behind I actually was.
Q16: Deadlines were supposed to motivate me, but they often just stressed me out and then passed. I'd scramble right before due dates, which explains my decent assignment scores when I actually submitted. But the exam caught me off guard—I couldn't cram my way through that. Deadlines without real engagement leading up to them are pretty useless.
Q17: I didn't seek out feedback much, and when I got it, my response was delayed. Good feedback on assignments made me feel briefly capable, but it didn't translate into better habits. The peer support group gave me informal feedback that was more impactful because it came from people in the same situation. Official feedback felt too distant.
Q18: The course structure assumed consistent engagement that I just couldn't maintain. Having so much content spread across the semester meant I could fall behind invisibly until it was too late. The exam weighting really hurt me—my assignment work was strong but one bad exam tanked everything. A different structure might have caught my drift earlier.
Q19: The heavy reliance on video content made engagement harder for someone like me who learns through reading and writing. The discussion requirements felt forced rather than organic. What helped was having downloadable materials and clear assignment rubrics. More flexibility in how to demonstrate learning would have suited me better.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat—I had decent activity in some ways but poor submission rates and a terrible exam score. Sometimes you're going through the motions without actually producing work. Or you're exploring, trying to understand, but never reaching the point where you can complete something. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That's interesting because it's almost the opposite of my situation. I can see how someone who studies effectively offline, with good materials and focus, wouldn't need the LMS much. If they attend lectures or have strong external resources, their low activity might not reflect their actual learning. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, time spent. But they miss the quality of that time entirely. They can't tell if you're deeply focused or mindlessly scrolling. They miss offline study, peer conversations, the thinking you do while walking around. They capture presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, asking questions, making connections. For me, true engagement happened in spurts, usually around assignment deadlines when I'd suddenly care deeply for a few days. Sustained engagement requires motivation I struggled to maintain.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my genuine interest in some topics that I explored outside the platform. It wouldn't show the conversations with my peer support group that kept me from dropping out entirely. It wouldn't show how surprised and disappointed I was when I realized I was failing despite thinking I was doing okay. The emotional journey is invisible in the data.
